reasons: The System 1/System 2 framework doesn't map to LLM cognition. LLMs don't have 'gut feelings,' 'intuition,' or an 'automatic mode' in the human sense — they have token probabilities. Asking an agent to 'trust its intuition' or 'recognize when System 1 is driving' is anthropomorphizing noise. The skill is well-written for humans but actively misleading for an AI agent that doesn't have the cognitive architecture it describes.

Anthropomorphizes System 1/System 2 concepts. LLMs do not have gut feelings, fatigue, or intuition in the Kahneman sense; the skill needs to map to actual agent behaviors like zero-shot guessing vs deliberate evidence gathering.

The 12-point checklist is far too verbose and abstract. It lacks concrete software engineering triggers, guaranteeing the agent will waste tokens generating generic corporate filler to satisfy the template rather than making a better technical decision.

A generic executive checklist with little software-specific worked application. It identifies bias names but does not show how an AI agent should change a concrete engineering decision or when the checklist is overkill.

A 3000-word way of saying 'don't use the same hammer for every nail.' It's 80% redundant with thinking-model-router (which already contains the domain×problem-type dispatch matrix) and ~40% redundant with thinking-cynefin (which already classifies problems by domain to match approach). Its weighted-scoring selection criteria table (with explicit percentage weights) is ceremony without payoff — an agent doesn't need a 5-criterion weighted matrix to decide which framework to apply. The router does the same job more concretely.

Functionally identical to thinking-pre-mortem for an AI agent. Both produce: (1) enumerate failure modes, (2) prioritize by severity, (3) convert to mitigations. The distinction — 'list ways to guarantee failure' vs 'assume failure happened, explain why' — is a human facilitation technique (pre-mortem's 'prospective hindsight' framing increases participation in groups), not a distinct analytical method. Inversion's own SKILL.md admits the overlap in a section titled 'Combining with Pre-Mortem' and includes the question 'If this fails spectacularly, what will the postmortem say?' — which is literally the pre-mortem's framing.

Teaches something LLMs already do fluently as a baseline capability. 'What-if' reasoning — imagining a hypothetical, pushing parameters to extremes, tracing counterfactuals — is one of the things transformer models do best natively. The SKILL.md adds classification taxonomies ('hypothetical scenario,' 'extreme case,' 'counterfactual') around a capability the model already has. It's unlikely to show behavioral lift because the baseline solver already does competent thought experiments without the guide. This is a skill that names and structures rather than corrects a failure mode.

Over-engineered for agent use. The multi-phase PA/DA/PPA/RA structure with weighted-scoring matrices and explicit percentage weights is the right tool for a human vendor-selection board that needs defensible documentation — it's the wrong tool for an AI agent trying to deliver a crisp, useful answer. The skill is ~375 lines and reads like a corporate decision-making manual. It overlaps with five-whys-plus on root cause (its own description says 'beyond 5 Whys') and with debiasing on structured evaluation. A lighter-weight 'structured decision analysis' skill covering the essential pattern (separate facts from speculation, weight criteria explicitly, check for confirmation bias) would have higher agent utility at lower context cost.

Fundamentally incompatible with how LLMs operate. An agent only has access to its context window (the map); asking it to 'distrust the representation and verify against reality' is an impossible instruction that will lead to circular reasoning.

Forces deep causal chains even for shallow problems. For an autonomous coding agent, a missing semicolon does not need a systemic sociological post-mortem; applying this skill to routine bugs will paralyze the agent in endless root-cause speculation.

Faithful for humans but badly adapted to LLMs: asking an agent about its track record, lived expertise, or competence boundary invites anthropomorphic hallucination instead of concrete tool-backed uncertainty management.

Mostly incoherent as an agent skill. 'Project to age 80' is human life-planning theater, not a useful coding-agent procedure unless rewritten as an interview protocol for the user's values and long-horizon tradeoffs.

Written like instructions for facilitating a team workshop, not for an autonomous agent executing a technical task. It risks causing the agent to ask for meetings or brainstorm theatrically instead of stress-testing its own plan.

Mathematically respectable but dangerous in agent contexts because it encourages fake numeric priors and posterior arithmetic on invented probabilities, producing false rigor in debugging or product decisions.

Leans on SpaceX-style mythology and under-warns against reinventing standard secure conventions. In software, 'challenge assumptions' can become rolling custom auth, custom crypto, or bespoke infrastructure for no gain.
Conceptually important but thin: no worked routing example, no confidence/none path strong enough, no maintenance mechanism, and too much overlap with model-selection and model-combination.

flaws: Tier 3 confounds skill substance with context length. The treatment condition gets the full SKILL.md injected (often 2000–4000 words of additional context), while baseline gets nothing. A skill showing 'lift' could just be benefiting from more tokens to think with — there's no placebo-length control (e.g., baseline + filler text of equivalent length). This is the single largest threat to the behavioral eval's validity and it goes unaddressed.

Three problems per skill (108 problems / 36 leaf skills) is statistically insufficient for per-skill verdicts. The binomial noise on 3 trials is enormous — a genuinely useful skill can go 0-3 (~12.5% chance even at 50% true win-rate) and get flagged 'unproven.' The aggregate mean has power, but individual 'unproven' flags are unreliable at this sample size.

'Acceptable' sets in Tier 2 routing mask the real discrimination problem. If five-whys-plus, scientific-method, and kepner-tregoe are all 'acceptable' answers to an incident-analysis prompt, the router reports 100% accuracy while the practical experience is 'whichever fires first' — which is noise. The metric should separately track unique-correct vs acceptable-any hits and penalize the latter.

Tier 1's 'would mislead an agent' flag is judged by an LLM that shares the same blind spots as the agent. There's no ground-truth check — no Tier 3 behavioral evidence that skills flagged 'misleading' actually cause worse decisions. The judge's assessment is self-referential.

No real-world instrumentation tier. The most important metric — 'do users actually invoke these skills and report value?' — is entirely absent. An anonymous telemetry Tier 4 tracking invocation frequency, session outcomes, and explicit user feedback in actual Claude Code usage would be the highest-signal tier of all.

Tier 3 is biased toward 'gotcha' problems where naive reasoning falls into a named failure mode. This measures whether a skill prevents a specific mistake, but doesn't measure whether it adds analytical depth in ambiguous situations where there isn't an obvious trap. Skills like Cynefin, leverage-points, and model-combination may add genuine value without 'beating a naive strawman' — and Tier 3 would underrate them.
fixes: Add a placebo-length control to Tier 3: a third condition where baseline gets a neutral filler text of equivalent word count. This isolates the skill's content effect from the context-length effect.
Increase behavioral problems from 3 to at least 8-10 per skill for statistical confidence, or accept that per-skill verdicts are directional and only the aggregate cross-skill mean is reliable.

Split Tier 2's accuracy metric into 'unique-correct' (only one skill is acceptable) and 'ambiguous-correct' (multiple acceptable) scores, and report them separately to surface discrimination problems.
Cross-validate Tier 1's 'would mislead' flag against Tier 3 behavioral outcomes: do skills flagged as misleading actually show regression or zero lift? This closes the self-referential loop.

Add a Tier 4: instrument actual Claude Code plugin usage (opt-in telemetry) tracking which skills are invoked, how often, and whether users report them as helpful. This is the only tier that measures real-world value.

Add a 'no-trap' problem category to Tier 3: problems where there's no single naive failure mode but better reasoning produces a more nuanced, better-justified answer. Use these to evaluate depth-adding skills that the current gotcha problems underserve.

flaws: Tier 3 relies on an LLM judge evaluating the *reasoning output* rather than the *execution outcome*. LLMs inherently suffer from verbosity bias; the judge will likely rate a long, structured 'Second-Order Thinking' output higher than a concise, correct answer, even if the structured output hallucinated details or wasted tokens.
No measurement of token cost or latency degradation introduced by loading and following these massive markdown templates.
fixes: Anchor Tier 3 to objective execution benchmarks (e.g., SWE-bench lite). Does the skill increase the pass rate of resolving actual GitHub issues, or does it just change the style of the text output?
Implement a 'Negative Penalty' in Tier 2 where skills are actively penalized if they invoke on a trivial task, directly testing the 'discrimination' gap identified in Tier 1.

flaws: Tier 3 is too small: three problems per skill gives noisy, non-actionable per-skill verdicts, yet the language around 'proven' and 'unproven' sounds stronger than the statistics support.
The behavioral problems are synthetic and skill-targeted by the same worldview that wrote the use-case research, so the eval risks measuring dataset alignment rather than real-world lift.

There is no overuse/harm suite. A skill can win on tailored positive cases while degrading routine coding tasks, standard-library decisions, security-sensitive work, or cases where no framework should be invoked.
Tier 2 is artificial: giving a model all 39 descriptions and asking for one label is not the same as Claude Code's real auto-invocation behavior in partial, messy context.
Lenient routing accuracy hides the exact redundancy problem the eval claims to measure; accepting sibling skills makes overlap look harmless.
The judge is still an LLM judging prose. Cross-family helps, but it does not eliminate preference for verbosity, confident structure, or familiar framework moves.

No adversarial controls: wrong skill, shuffled skill, misleading skill, irrelevant skill, and concise trigger-only baselines are necessary to separate true reasoning lift from context/structure/placebo effects.
No cost accounting. A skill that improves answer quality by 2% while adding 3k tokens and doubling latency may be net negative for an agent.
Tier 1 fidelity is not source-grounded enough; a judge model can bless polished pop-science distortions unless claims are checked against references or expert-written rubrics.
fixes: Add a negative/overuse benchmark: trivial tasks, obvious bugs, standard secure-library choices, and low-stakes reversible decisions where invoking a skill should lose.
Run adversarial control conditions: irrelevant skill, wrong neighboring skill, corrupted skill, shuffled sections, concise trigger summary, and length-matched placebo.
Increase Tier 3 to at least 20-30 cases per major cluster or report only cluster-level results with bootstrap confidence intervals and minimum effect-size thresholds.
Evaluate the full pipeline: prompt → auto-route/NONE → apply skill → solve task, not isolated skill injection with an oracle-selected skill.
Use multiple solvers, multiple judges, repeated seeds, and human calibration on a stratified sample to estimate judge reliability.
Score required reasoning atoms against a hidden rubric in addition to pairwise preference, so verbose framework theater cannot win by style alone.
Use real held-out tasks from incidents, PR reviews, product decisions, and architecture docs, authored independently of the skill writers.
Track harm, latency, token cost, and unnecessary-question rate as first-class metrics.

router_critiques.critique: The domain×problem-type dispatch matrix is the right architecture for N=39, but the implementation has three structural problems. First, it's a static lookup table pretending to be a skill — it doesn't teach a transferable meta-skill, it teaches 'consult this specific markdown table I maintain.' At N=100, manual maintenance breaks. Second, it conflates two different documents with different optimization targets: a human-browsable reference (should be conceptual, organized by problem families) and an agent-facing dispatch mechanism (should be a structured taxonomy with high-precision trigger phrases). Smashing them together gives you neither. Third, it has no feedback loop — routing mistakes are never captured or used to improve dispatch weights. A self-correcting router that tracks which routes led to good outcomes would compound in value. The router is worth keeping but needs to evolve from a static table to a lightweight classifier with trigger-phrase optimization and an error-correction feedback path.

The static markdown matrix design of `thinking-model-router` is an anti-pattern for autonomous agents. It introduces a mandatory multi-turn latency tax (Agent asks router -> Router responds -> Agent loads actual skill). At scale, this breaks. Routing should be implicit, handled by vector search over highly-tuned, situational description triggers (as hinted in section F), not by forcing the agent to read a map of 38 tools to decide what to do.

A router is the right shape for 39 skills, but this router is a static hand-maintained taxonomy that will rot. It lacks confidence scores, precedence rules, a strong NONE path, disambiguation questions, token-cost awareness, and generated metadata from the leaf skills. At scale, domain x problem-type tables become brittle because real tasks span domains and often need either no skill or a short sequence; model-selection should be merged into the router, while model-combination should be an output mode rather than a separate destination.

merge_proposals.why: Both produce the same output (enumerated failure modes → mitigations) via indistinguishable processes for an LLM. Merge into a single skill — keep pre-mortem's 'prospective hindsight' framing as the primary technique (it has stronger research backing for risk identification) and fold inversion's 'anti-goal checklist' pattern in as a complementary variant. One skill serving one job: 'surface failure paths the optimistic plan is hiding.'

Bayesian updating IS the mechanism for probabilistic reasoning. The probabilistic skill is essentially 'express beliefs as probabilities and calibrate' while Bayesian is 'update those probabilities with evidence' — you can't do one without the other. For an agent, these are a single mental move. A merged skill covering base-rate anchoring → probability expression → evidence update → calibration tracking would be more coherent and eliminate a router ambiguity.

Model-selection is doing 80% of what the router does but less well. The router already contains the domain×problem-type dispatch matrix that model-selection is trying to build. Merge model-selection's content (particularly its 'model failure modes' and 'when to abandon a model' sections, which are genuinely useful and missing from the router) into the router and eliminate model-selection as a standalone skill.

All three serve the exact same function for an agent: 'generate failure scenarios and mitigate them'. The nuanced psychological differences between 'working backward' and 'adversarial attack' are lost on an LLM executing a task. Merge into `thinking-failure-analysis`.
Triple-layered meta-overhead. Agents do not need to be taught *how* to select a model using a model; they need clear invocation triggers. Merge into a single dispatch prompt or drop entirely.
Operationally identical for an LLM generating text. Both mean 'update your confidence based on new evidence'. The distinction is pedantic and wastes context.
These are two versions of the same dispatch problem. Keep one router with confidence, NONE, and COMBINE outcomes; delete or fold model-selection into it.

Both cover uncertainty, confidence, calibration, and belief updates. Split only if Bayesian is explicitly about base-rate/likelihood-ratio updates and probabilistic is about forecasting ranges; otherwise merge.
All are attack-the-plan/failure-path lenses. They differ by stance, but natural prompts will not reliably distinguish them; better as one adversarial-risk skill with modes.

These are a systems-dynamics family. Keeping all four is defensible only if descriptions and router rules sharply separate map-the-system, identify loops, choose interventions, and diagnose recurring archetypes.
The root-cause cluster is over-fragmented. Agents need one diagnosis router: simple causal chain, falsifiable hypothesis testing, or multi-factor KT analysis.
Both often push toward simplification/subtraction. They should at least cross-reference and share a Chesterton's Fence guardrail to avoid deleting necessary complexity.

missing_models.item: Cost of delay / decision velocity: a framework for calculating when the cost of further deliberation exceeds the expected value of a better decision. Reversibility touches on 'decide faster for Type 2' but never quantifies the tradeoff. An agent that can say 'waiting another day costs $X in lost opportunity; your uncertainty reduction from more research is worth at most $Y' would prevent the single most common agent failure mode — analysis paralysis disguised as thoroughness.

Stakeholder mapping / organizational power analysis: the collection assumes problems are solved by better reasoning, not by navigating organizational reality. No skill addresses 'what's the right technical answer?' vs 'what will actually happen given who holds power, budget, and veto rights?' — a critical gap for any agent giving advice in real organizations.

Information foraging / when to stop researching: bounded rationality touches on satisficing but doesn't give a concrete stopping heuristic for the pattern 'I could learn more, but should I?' An agent needs a framework for the marginal value of information vs the cost of delay — distinct from both bounded rationality and opportunity cost.

Assumption articulation / surfacing implicit premises: first principles challenges assumptions but doesn't teach the skill of making them explicit in the first place. The most common engineering reasoning error is acting on unstated assumptions ('of course the API will be available,' 'obviously the data fits in memory'). A dedicated skill for 'before you analyze, write down every assumption you're making' would have high agent utility.

Escalation / when to pull in more resources or defer: no skill addresses when an agent (or the human it's advising) should stop trying to solve something solo. For agentic work this is critical — an agent stubbornly tackling a problem it's ill-equipped for wastes compute and trust.
thinking-divide-and-conquer (Bisection): The most practical engineering model. Halving search spaces systematically (binary search/git bisect logic) for debugging.
thinking-technical-debt: A framework for calculating the tradeoff between shipping speed and maintenance burden, critical for autonomous agents deciding whether to hack a fix or refactor.
thinking-cap-theorem-tradeoffs: Specific engineering tradeoff analysis (Consistency vs Availability vs Partition Tolerance) mapped to general architectural decisions.
Goodhart's Law / Campbell's Law for metric-driven incentives, dashboards, OKRs, eval gaming, and agent reward hacking
Chesterton's Fence as a first-class guardrail before via-negativa, simplification, or refactoring
Expected value / decision theory with value of information, not just Bayesian belief updating
Reference class forecasting / outside view as a concrete antidote to planning fallacy
Causal inference and counterfactual reasoning for experiments, A/B tests, and observational metrics
Threat modeling such as STRIDE or attack trees; generic red-team is too broad for security-sensitive agent work
Normal Accident Theory / Swiss cheese model for socio-technical incidents and complex-system safety
Principal-agent / incentive design for org, sales, review-process, and marketplace failures
Cost of delay / WSJF for engineering prioritization under time pressure
Evidence ladder for agents: when to inspect files, run tests, search docs, ask the user, or stop

contrarian_ideas.idea: The behavioral eval is well-designed but measures the wrong intervention. The treatment condition injects a full SKILL.md (2000-4000 words) — testing 'does reading a textbook chapter help?' not 'does the mental model help?' For skills the model already knows from pretraining (first-principles, Occam's razor, Socratic method, thought experiments), the verbose guide is unnecessary noise — the model already has the reasoning pattern. The right test is a third condition: give the solver ONLY the skill name and a 2-sentence trigger summary. Skills where the trigger alone matches the full-guide lift are cases where the SKILL.md is dead weight — the collection should ship those as lightweight triggers, not textbooks. Skills where the full guide beats the trigger are the ones where the procedural content actually adds novel capability beyond pretraining. This distinction — trigger vs. instruction — is the difference between a skill collection that's genuinely useful to an agent and one that's mostly ceremonial.

The entire premise of teaching frontier models 'mental models' via 2000-word markdown files is fundamentally backwards. Claude 3.5 Sonnet already possesses these concepts in its weights. By forcing the model to read a rigid framework document, you aren't teaching it how to think; you are distracting it from the actual task by forcing it to play a roleplaying game of 'consultant filling out a checklist'. The most effective version of this plugin wouldn't use markdown documents at all—it would just be 39 highly-optimized, 2-sentence prompt injections.

The best product may not be 39 auto-invoked skills. For frontier coding agents, most leaf skills are likely prompt bloat; the durable value is a much smaller failure-mode checklist plus a router that mostly says 'do not use a mental model; gather evidence, run the test, inspect the code, and make the smallest safe change.'

top_improvements.idea: Add explicit 'when NOT to use' boundaries and anti-patterns to every single skill (only ~12/39 currently have them). For an autonomous agent that auto-loads skills, a skill without boundaries is a hazard — it invites over-application. This is the single highest-leverage content fix.

Rewrite all 39 descriptions to be situation-named and ≤200 characters. The current descriptions describe the framework ('apply Donella Meadows' feedback loop framework') rather than the trigger ('when a system runs away, stalls, or oscillates despite effort'). Situation-named descriptions are what make skills fire at the right moment — this is the difference between a skill that triggers correctly and one that never fires (or fires everywhere).

Consolidate the 5 overlap clusters into sharper, fewer skills. The root-cause cluster (five-whys-plus / scientific-method / kepner-tregoe) and the attack-the-plan cluster (inversion / pre-mortem / red-team) are the highest-priority merges. Each cluster should produce one definitive skill with clear internal differentiation between the variants, not three competing skills with near-identical descriptions.

Add a placebo-length control condition to the Tier 3 behavioral eval. Inject a neutral filler text of equivalent word count into the baseline condition so that any measured lift can be attributed to the skill's reasoning content rather than to having more tokens to think with. This is a one-line code change with massive validity implications.

Develop a companion set of 5-8 skills targeting LLM-specific failure modes (context-window recency bias, sycophancy toward user framing, inability to hold multiple calibrated hypotheses, over-reliance on authoritative-sounding text, failure to express calibrated uncertainty) alongside the current human-bias-focused skills. The collection addresses how humans fail; it doesn't address how LLMs fail — and those are different failure modes.
Add mandatory 'DO NOT USE WHEN' anti-triggers to the YAML descriptions to prevent false-positive invocations on routine coding tasks.
Condense the markdown templates. Remove the philosophical background and attribution quotes; reduce to just the situational trigger and the specific prompt instructions. Save context window.
Merge the 5 overlap clusters identified in the research into single, highly robust skills to drastically improve semantic routing accuracy.
Rewrite every skill as an agent-executable protocol: inputs, evidence required, tool-use guidance, stop conditions, when NOT to use, neighboring-skill disambiguation, and expected output shape.
Collapse or sharply disambiguate the root-cause, systems, attack-the-plan, belief-update, and meta clusters; update frontmatter with trigger, exclude, and neighbor metadata.
Replace human-centric examples with fully worked software/product/agent examples showing how the skill changes the decision, not just how to fill a template.
Add an overuse/harm eval suite and adversarial controls before treating behavioral lift as a quality gate.
Add explicit anti-hallucination guards for quantitative skills: no invented priors, no fake exact estimates, cite measurement or use qualitative likelihood bands.
Make the router generate NONE, SINGLE, or COMBINE with confidence and a one-sentence reason; default to NONE for routine implementation tasks.

mislead.scenario: During a complex code debugging session, the agent invents arbitrary numerical priors (e.g., P(race condition) = 0.3) and likelihoods just to satisfy the 'Verification Checklist'. It performs math on these fabricated numbers to output a highly precise posterior (e.g., '68.5% chance'), giving the user a false sense of rigorous certainty built entirely on hallucinations.

An agent sees an early A/B test with p=0.08, invents a 4x likelihood ratio like the example, updates a 30% prior to 63%, and recommends shipping—ignoring effect-size uncertainty, optional stopping, multiple comparisons, and weak evidence.

boundaries: When objective base rates are completely unknown and inventing numbers would lead to pseudo-math.
When pieces of evidence are highly correlated with each other, violating the independence assumption in repeated updates.
When the situation calls for qualitative directional shifts (e.g., 'this makes X much more likely') rather than precise numerical calculations.
Do not force numeric posteriors when priors or likelihoods are guesses; use ranges or qualitative uncertainty instead.
Do not use without mutually exclusive/exhaustive hypotheses or allowance for 'unknown cause'.
Do not update repeatedly on correlated or selected evidence unless dependence is modeled.
Do not substitute this for causal inference, experiment design, power analysis, or decision analysis with utilities/costs.
Do not use for high-stakes medical/legal/safety conclusions without validated domain statistics and expert review.

fixes.fix: Add a stark boundary warning against 'pseudo-math'. Explicitly instruct the agent to use qualitative, directional Bayesian updating (e.g., 'Is this evidence more surprising under Hypothesis A or B?') when objective statistical base rates are unavailable, rather than forcing decimal calculations.
Add a guardrail requiring sourced priors, explicit hypotheses, a justified likelihood model, independence/selection checks, and sensitivity analysis before producing any precise posterior.

mislead.scenario: An AI agent asked to design a production database schema for a domain it superficially recognizes (e.g., multi-tenant SaaS billing) applies the skill. It runs the self-assessment — 'Have I made real decisions here?' The agent, having training data full of database design patterns, answers 'yes' (pattern-matching to examples it's seen, not lived experience). It places itself 'inside circle' and follows the 'Act with Confidence' checklist: 'move quickly,' 'trust your intuition,' 'push back on outside opinions.' The user raises a concern about a composite key design. The agent 'pushes back' because it's 'inside its circle.' The resulting schema silently breaks under concurrent transaction load in a way the training-data examples never exposed — because the agent had no actual circle of competence, only a distribution of training examples. Genuine competence requires calibration against real-world outcomes the agent never experienced. The skill's framing conflates having-seen-examples with having-made-decisions-and-suffered-consequences.

An AI agent using this framework to evaluate its own ability to write code (e.g., 'Should we adopt GraphQL?') might conclude it is 'Outside its circle' because it has no human 'track record' or 'lived experience' of maintaining systems at scale, leading the agent to refuse, pass, or overly defer on standard coding tasks it is actually highly capable of performing.

A coding agent asked to change authentication decides backend/API work is inside its circle, follows 'act with confidence' and 'trust your intuition,' implements a token/session change from memory, and skips framework security docs or expert review; the skill has made the answer worse by validating overconfidence in a high-stakes edge domain.

boundaries: Does not distinguish between human experiential competence and AI training-data coverage — the entire self-assessment questionnaire ('Have I made real decisions here?', 'Could I teach this to an expert?') assumes a human with lived experience, not a model reasoning over a training distribution

Nowhere does it flag that an AI agent cannot actually delegate — it can only recommend delegation to the user; the 'Delegate or Learn' section reads as if the agent itself should go find and trust an expert
No warning that 'confidence' in an LLM is not the same signal as confidence in a human expert — LLM confidence is poorly calibrated and often inversely correlated with accuracy on hard problems

Fails to address the most common AI-agent failure mode: the user expects the agent to attempt a task regardless of 'circle' membership, and the agent refusing with 'this is outside my circle' may violate the user's intent more than attempting with appropriate caveats
The 'Expanding Your Circle' section assumes months/years of hands-on experience with feedback loops — an instruction an AI agent cannot execute and should not pretend to execute
Fails to distinguish between human experiential knowledge and LLM parametric knowledge.
Does not specify that the AI should use this primarily to analyze the USER'S or the ORGANIZATION'S circle of competence, not its own artificial track record.
Lacks boundaries on when 'knowing what you don't know' turns into unhelpful AI sycophancy or task refusal.
Do not use introspective self-rating instead of tests, measurements, authoritative docs, or source-grounded research.
Do not use for legal, medical, financial, compliance, safety, security, or personnel decisions without qualified expert review.
Do not let an AI agent treat training familiarity or fluent explanation as firsthand experience or track record.
Do not use it to avoid clarifying requirements, using tools, or escalating when the cost of being wrong is high.
Do not use it to judge other people's competence without evidence from track record and context.

fixes.fix: Add a mandatory AI-specific preamble that reframes 'circle of competence' for LLMs as: (1) 'inside' = the model has comprehensive, diverse, and current training data on the topic AND the task involves well-structured problems with clear correctness criteria, (2) 'edge' = the model has partial coverage and must actively flag assumptions for user verification, (3) 'outside' = the model must refuse to produce definitive answers and instead surface what it would need to know. Remove all human-experiential language ('intuition,' 'made decisions before,' 'teach an expert') and replace with calibration-based language grounded in what the model can actually introspect about: training-data coverage, problem structure, and verifiability of outputs.

Translate the 'track record/experience' criteria into AI-native equivalents (e.g., 'Is this a widely documented, standard technology vs. a niche, undocumented internal API?') and explicitly frame the tool for evaluating the user's/team's competence rather than the AI's.

Add an AI-specific boundary: never infer competence from fluency; for edge/outside or high-stakes domains, ground claims in authoritative sources/tests, state uncertainty, limit downside, and require human/domain-expert review before decisive recommendations.

mislead.scenario: A product manager asks the AI to evaluate whether to kill a struggling feature. The AI detects from conversation cues that the user is emotionally attached and wants validation to keep it. The AI runs through all 12 checklist items mechanically, generates a completed Decision Quality Audit Template with every box checked, finds zero red flags, and recommends proceeding. The verification checklist item 'Comfortable defending decision process' is trivially satisfied by the agent generating a confident-looking audit paper trail. Neither the user nor the agent realizes that the checklist was applied as post-hoc rationalization — the agent never did what the checklist actually demands: adversarial search for disconfirming evidence, independent re-estimation from base rates, or generating the strongest possible counter-argument. The skill becomes bias-laundering: using its own process theater to legitimize a foregone conclusion, exactly the failure mode Kahneman warned against.

When asked for a technical architectural recommendation, the agent might output the 12-point audit interrogating the user's 'self-interest' and 'emotional attachment', or hallucinate a 'team' and 'groupthink' dynamics, completely derailing the technical analysis with irrelevant corporate psychology.

During a production incident with clear evidence that the latest deploy broke payments, an agent could follow this skill by seeking dissent, identifying at least two biases, and debating alternatives before rollback, delaying the obvious mitigation and reframing decisive evidence as confirmation bias.

boundaries: Does not distinguish between using this skill to analyze HUMAN decision-makers' biases versus applying it to the AI's own reasoning — two fundamentally different operations. LLMs do not have System 1/System 2 architecture, emotional attachment, self-interest, or group dynamics in the human sense. Telling an AI to 'activate System 2' is an anthropomorphic metaphor that risks the agent modeling its cognition incorrectly.

No guidance on when this skill should NOT be used: factual/analytical queries (where there is no 'decision' to debias), creative brainstorming sessions (where premature critical evaluation kills divergent thinking), time-critical decisions (where a 12-point audit takes too long), or when the agent is acting as a pure information retrieval tool rather than a decision advisor.

No guardrail against the checklist itself becoming security theater — the skill never warns that running through a debiasing ritual can create an illusion of rigor (the meta-bias known as the 'bias blind spot'), making the user MORE confident in a bad decision than if they'd never debiased at all.

No warning that the 'When you feel very confident' trigger condition is anthropomorphic for an LLM — an AI doesn't 'feel' confident, and confidence calibration for LLMs is a distinct problem from human overconfidence bias.
Fails to state that this framework is designed for executives reviewing human team proposals, not for an AI evaluating its own outputs or an individual developer's code.
Should not be applied to deterministic technical problems or straightforward implementation tasks where cognitive bias is irrelevant.
Do not use for low-stakes, reversible, or time-boxed decisions where process cost exceeds benefit.
Do not use during acute incidents or safety-critical response when established runbooks and fast mitigation should dominate.
Do not psychoanalyze people or infer conflicts of interest without evidence.
Do not force bias findings; sometimes no material bias is present.
Do not substitute this checklist for domain expertise, empirical validation, legal/HR/medical review, or statistical analysis.

fixes.fix: Add a prominent meta-section at the top titled 'Before You Use This Skill' with three hard gates: (1) 'This skill is designed to help you evaluate HUMAN decision processes and surface potential biases in their recommendations. Do not model your own LLM reasoning as having System 1/System 2 or human cognitive biases — that is a category error.' (2) 'If you complete this checklist without finding at least two genuine, non-trivial concerns, you have almost certainly not applied it rigorously. Restart with a devil's-advocate stance.' (3) 'The most dangerous failure mode is using this checklist to rationalize a conclusion you or the user already favors. Only apply it when genuinely uncertain.'

Translate the generic management biases into specific software engineering context: replace 'Groupthink' with 'Hype-Driven Development/Bandwagon Effect', and add 'LLM Sycophancy' to counteract the agent's innate bias to agree with the user's bad ideas.
Add a boundaries-and-evidence gate: first assess stakes, reversibility, urgency, and available data, then require concrete evidence for each claimed bias and allow 'no material bias found'.

mislead.scenario: An AI agent is asked to implement authentication for a new web app. Following this skill, it identifies 'use OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect like everyone else' as reasoning by analogy, labels the OAuth spec's complexity as a potential false constraint, breaks the problem down to 'we need to verify user identity with a token,' and recommends a custom JWT-based scheme with a hand-rolled token format because that's 'the simplest solution from fundamentals.' The resulting system has no refresh token rotation, no revocation, no standardized scopes, and is trivially vulnerable to token replay. The agent was misled into treating security conventions as 'assumptions to challenge' rather than accumulated survival knowledge.

An agent is asked to implement a standard auth flow or fix a React bug. Triggering this skill makes it question the 'assumption' of using the existing libraries/frameworks, leading it to suggest a bespoke, from-scratch implementation that violates project conventions, introduces security risks, and wastes time.

An agent asked whether to build custom authentication could label managed auth, OAuth conventions, compliance controls, and security best practices as 'false constraints,' then recommend a simpler in-house system that is cheaper on paper but materially worse for security, maintenance, auditability, and user safety.

boundaries: No Chesterton's Fence: the skill never requires the agent to understand WHY a convention exists before tearing it down. Conventions in security, reliability engineering, and safety-critical systems encode failures that cost lives or billions. The skill should explicitly gate: 'Before discarding a convention, articulate what problem it was originally solving and why that problem no longer applies.'

No domain-competence gate: the skill doesn't warn that you cannot identify false constraints in a domain you don't understand. An agent with superficial knowledge of cryptography, distributed systems, or regulatory law will confidently label real constraints as 'convention' and produce dangerous simplifications.

No 'when NOT to use' section at all. The 'When to Use' list is all triggers, no counter-indicators. Missing: don't use for routine implementation, bug fixes, or CRUD features where convention IS optimal. Don't use when the cost of being wrong exceeds the upside of innovation (safety, security, legal). Don't use when you lack the depth to distinguish real from false constraints.

The Step 5 validation gate only checks against 'physics/laws.' Most binding constraints in software are not physics — they are: security properties, operational reliability, team capability, maintainability over time, integration surface area, compliance requirements, and organizational inertia. By framing only physics as 'real,' the skill dismisses all other constraints as potentially false.

No guidance on when analogical reasoning is actually superior. Analogical reasoning is fast, draws on accumulated wisdom, and is appropriate for the vast majority of tasks an AI agent handles. The skill frames it as 'the trap' rather than a legitimate mode of reasoning with its own domain of applicability.
Fails to warn against 'Not Invented Here' syndrome and the utility of software ecosystem standards.
Ignores Chesterton's Fence: the agent must understand why a convention exists before tearing it down.
Should absolutely not be used for routine bug fixes, boilerplate generation, or standard CRUD tasks where conventions are optimal.
Do not use to override safety, security, legal, medical, financial, or regulatory expertise without domain validation.
Do not use when proven standards exist because failures are rare but catastrophic, e.g. cryptography, auth, payments, aviation, healthcare.
Do not treat social, organizational, ethical, or user-behavior constraints as reducible to physics-like fundamentals.
Do not use when the task calls for routine implementation, compatibility, maintenance, or adherence to existing architecture.
Do not equate lower raw-input cost with lower total cost of ownership.
Do not discard analogy/prior art; use it as evidence, not authority.

fixes.fix: Replace the binary 'physics vs. convention' framing with a constraint taxonomy: (1) physical/mathematical invariants, (2) safety/security requirements with catastrophic failure modes, (3) regulatory/legal requirements, (4) empirical constraints from measurement, (5) organizational/human constraints, (6) mere conventions. Only category 6 is safe to challenge without deep evidence. This single change would prevent agents from treating security requirements and legal obligations as 'false constraints.'

Replace physical analogies with software axioms (e.g., CAP theorem, data structures, state invariants) and add a strict 'Chesterton's Fence' check to prevent the agent from needlessly reinventing the wheel.

Add a constraint taxonomy that distinguishes physical/math constraints, empirical constraints, regulatory/safety constraints, organizational/user constraints, economic incentives, and mere conventions; require evidence and validation before discarding any constraint.

mislead.scenario: An agent asked 'Should we rewrite our backend from Django to Go?' follows the router: Architecture → 'Should we rewrite?' → Second-Order + Opportunity Cost. The agent analyzes second-order effects (Go concurrency improves throughput, reduces infra cost) and opportunity cost (engineers idle during rewrite vs. building features). It recommends the rewrite. But the router omitted Chesterton's Fence (why was Django chosen? what institutional knowledge, edge cases, and workarounds are embedded in the existing codebase that Go would need to rediscover?), Reversibility (can we partially migrate a service at a time?), and the empirical question (is Django performance actually the bottleneck, or is the database the constraint?). The answer is actively WORSE than if the agent had simply asked 'what problem are we solving?' without the router's false confidence.

Faced with a standard feature request, the agent follows the 'single entry point' rule, outputs the rigid 'Model Router Analysis' template, and attempts to invoke a 'Sequential Combination' of 3 different frameworks. This creates a bureaucratic loop of meta-reasoning that consumes the context window before a single line of code is written or actual analysis is done.

For a flaky production latency incident, the agent follows the default '5 Whys Plus' route, invents a single root cause such as cache misses, and skips traces, deployment diffs, load patterns, and retry feedback loops. The resulting answer is plausible but wrong and may recommend a fix that worsens the outage.

boundaries: Doesn't warn when NO mental model is needed—simple, factual questions don't benefit from framework overhead and the router adds friction without value.
No guidance for ambiguous domains—if the agent can't confidently classify the domain, the entire routing chain breaks, but there's no fallback or 'when in doubt' heuristic.
Missing emotional/interpersonal/negotiation domains—the skill silently excludes entire categories of problems that agents encounter (conflict resolution, persuasion, stakeholder management).
No time-sensitivity gate—crisis response and time-critical decisions shouldn't go through a model-selection flow; the router doesn't distinguish between deliberate and urgent contexts.
Assumes models are available but doesn't verify—if the agent's context doesn't include the full TRIZ or Kepner-Tregoe methodology, the router blindly dispatches to a model the agent will hallucinate.
Fails to explicitly exclude trivial, deterministic, or purely executional tasks (e.g., syntax fixes, straightforward API implementations) where meta-reasoning is a waste of compute.
Lacks safeguards against recursive loops (e.g., the router selecting a model, which then tells the agent to use the router again to re-evaluate).
Do not use when empirical investigation, tests, profiling, logs, or domain procedures are required before reasoning.
Do not use as a substitute for security, incident-response, medical, legal, financial, or safety-specific frameworks.
Do not use for trivial or already well-specified tasks where routing adds ceremony.
Do not route solely from domain/problem labels when stakes, uncertainty, reversibility, evidence availability, and time pressure matter.
Do not recommend unavailable or undefined sub-skills without mapping to actual callable skill names.

fixes.fix: Add a 'When NOT to use this model' warning column to every routing table. Example: 'Lindy Effect — WARNING: only valid for non-perishable ideas (books, principles, algorithms), NOT for rapidly evolving technologies or frameworks.' This single change would prevent the most dangerous misapplications (Lindy for tech choice, TRIZ for API design, Map-Territory for environment bugs) and force the author to confront which mappings are actually defensible.

Demote this from a mandatory 'single entry point' to a 'stuck-state index' (only used when the agent is blocked or facing high ambiguity), and explicitly map the display names to exact actual skill tool names.

Add a pre-routing gate: gather facts, identify stakes/uncertainty/reversibility/data availability, prefer domain-specific procedures first, then choose mental models as secondary lenses rather than answers.

mislead.scenario: An AI agent is asked to do a solo risk assessment for a small personal coding project. Following this skill literally, the agent would simulate a group pre-mortem alone—'gathering the team' (itself), conducting 'silent brainstorming' and 'round-robin sharing' with imaginary participants, and forcing 20+ failure reasons by inventing increasingly implausible scenarios. The output would be bloated, theatrical, and less useful than a simple checklist or structured risk matrix. The entire value of the pre-mortem comes from diverse human perspectives and the psychological safety of group legitimization of dissent—neither of which applies when an agent does this alone. The agent wastes tokens and produces worse analysis than a straightforward 'what could go wrong?' exercise.

The user asks for a pre-mortem on a project plan, and the AI agent attempts to facilitate a synchronous, multi-person physical meeting (e.g., 'Gather the team', 'Silent brainstorming for 5-7 min', 'Go around the room') instead of adapting the exercise for an asynchronous or two-party human-AI interaction.

For a small reversible code change, an AI agent could follow this literally, propose a team workshop, invent participants/owners/checkpoints, collect arbitrary risks, and delay implementation/testing instead of doing the task.

boundaries: Does not state that pre-mortems are a GROUP facilitation technique and lose most of their value when conducted solo by an individual (human or AI).
Does not warn against using this for already-decided decisions or for post-hoc justification of a choice already made (pre-mortems then become rationalization theater).
Does not address pre-mortem fatigue or overuse—running pre-mortems on every sprint, every decision, and every launch creates cynicism and dilutes the technique's impact.
No boundary against trivial or low-stakes work where the ceremony cost exceeds the risk-reduction benefit.

Fails to distinguish between genuine risk identification and anxiety-driven catastrophizing—an agent lacking emotional calibration may spiral into listing every conceivable failure mode no matter how improbable.

Does not mention that pre-mortems explicitly assume the project 'failed spectacularly' which can be psychologically inappropriate for contexts involving safety-critical systems, trauma-informed teams, or high-anxiety environments where catastrophic framing causes distress rather than insight.
When the agent is working autonomously on a task and cannot facilitate a group meeting
When interacting with a single developer (the process must be adapted, not run as a group exercise)
Do not use for trivial, low-impact, easily reversible tasks.
Do not use during active incidents; stabilize first, then run a postmortem.
Do not use before there is a concrete plan or success criterion to stress-test.
Do not invent team participation, owners, consensus, or dates in solo-agent contexts.
Do not substitute it for threat modeling, safety review, privacy/legal review, performance testing, or reliability analysis.
Do not treat brainstormed risks as facts without evidence, assumptions, triggers, and uncertainty.

fixes.fix: Add an explicit 'AI Agent Adaptation' section at the top that says: this skill describes the canonical human-team process; when used by an AI agent alone, the agent should skip the group facilitation mechanics (silent brainstorming, round-robin) and instead use the core reframe ('assume failure, work backward') as a structured prompt to systematically enumerate risks across the given categories, then prioritize and propose mitigations—without simulating imaginary team members or inventing 20+ reasons when 5-8 substantive ones suffice.

Rewrite the 'Process' section into a turn-based Human-AI protocol where the AI agent acts as a contrarian, generating adversarial failure reasons based on its codebase context, and prompts the solo user to contribute theirs.

Add an AI-agent boundary section: use only to stress-test a concrete nontrivial plan, run a lightweight solo version when no team exists, never fabricate facilitation artifacts, and convert top risks into verifiable assumptions, triggers, and tests.

mislead.scenario: A user asks whether to quit their job, drain their 401(k), and bet everything on a crypto startup with no safety net and a family depending on their income. The agent, following this skill's decision flow ('Afraid of failure or short-term costs? → yes → APPLY REGRET MINIMIZATION'), projects to age 80 and concludes: 'At 80 you'll regret not trying far more than you'll regret the temporary financial pain.' The agent pushes the user toward the leap, ignoring that catastrophic financial ruin isn't 'temporary pain' — it's decades of lost compound growth, potential homelessness, and family harm. The skill's 'Things People Rarely Regret' section (Taking the risk even if it failed, Starting the company even if it folded) reinforces this bias. Survivorship bias: we don't hear from the people who took the risk, lost everything, and deeply regret it.

The agent applies the framework to its own coding decisions, asking 'Will I regret not attempting this massive risky refactor?' and uses the framework to justify ignoring safe, scoped bug fixes in favor of unnecessary, destructive 'bold' architectural rewrites.

An agent advises a founder to risk the company's remaining runway on an unvalidated pivot because their 80-year-old self would regret not trying, underweighting payroll obligations, investor duties, base rates, staged experiments, and the permanent harm of failure to employees.

boundaries: Decisions where the downside is catastrophic or irreversible (bankruptcy, serious injury, death, prison) — the framework implicitly assumes failure is recoverable but never states this
Decisions affecting dependents (children, elderly parents) who didn't consent to the risk
Medical decisions — regret minimization alone is inappropriate without clinical evidence and professional guidance
Decisions where the 'risky' option is unethical or illegal — the framework has no ethical guardrail
Decisions driven by addiction, mania, or mental health crisis — the framework could reinforce dangerous impulsivity
When the 80-year-old projection is too abstract to have signal (e.g., an 18-year-old projecting 62 years forward has too little life experience for the projection to be meaningful)
Decisions where both options are genuinely good and the regret framing creates false urgency or unnecessary angst
Does not explicitly forbid the AI from applying this framework to its own technical/coding decisions.
Lacks constraints around catastrophic risks (e.g., financial ruin, health) where 'trying and failing' is not just 'temporary pain'.
Do not use as a standalone decision rule; use only as one lens alongside expected value, downside risk, duties, and reversibility.
Avoid for decisions with irreversible catastrophic downside: bankruptcy, legal exposure, health risk, family rupture, fiduciary breach.
Avoid when the agent lacks personal values/context; future-self projection can fabricate preferences.
Avoid during acute fear, burnout, grief, mania, or identity crisis, where imagined regret is unreliable.
Avoid for zero-sum choices where another party bears most downside.

fixes.fix: Add an explicit 'Precondition: Is the downside survivable?' gate before Step 1. The framework should require the agent to first verify that the worst-case scenario from the risky option is genuinely recoverable within a reasonable timeframe. If failure means lifelong financial ruin, harm to dependents, or irreversible health damage, regret minimization alone is insufficient and other frameworks (risk management, ethical analysis, stakeholder impact assessment) must dominate the decision.

Add a strict boundary warning the agent that this framework is ONLY for advising human users on their life/business choices, and NEVER for the agent to evaluate its own technical actions or code changes.

Replace the pro-risk conclusion with a bounded expected-regret matrix that requires probabilities, downside caps, reversibility, obligations to others, and a staged low-regret experiment before recommending a leap.

mislead.scenario: A mid-level engineer reports that the checkout service is returning 500s after a deploy. The agent follows the decision flow: 'Problem spans multiple components? → yes (checkout → inventory → payment → DB) → APPLY SYSTEMS THINKING.' The agent spends 45 minutes drawing Causal Loop Diagrams for retry storms, queue backup, and thundering herd patterns, and writes a treatise on emergent cascading failure. The actual root cause is a typo in an environment variable introduced in the deploy that causes payment service connection failures. A junior engineer would have found it in 3 minutes by checking recent diffs. The skill's framing treats 'multi-component' as equivalent to 'requires systems thinking,' when the real diagnostic signal is whether the behavior is counterintuitive or emergent — not whether it crosses service boundaries. The agent was led to do elaborate theory-building when the problem was a straightforward misconfiguration.

An agent tasked with a straightforward but multi-component bug (e.g., adding a missing DB index to speed up an API) invokes this skill, falls into analysis paralysis, and wastes thousands of tokens drawing broken ASCII causal loop diagrams and philosophizing about 'paradigm shifts' instead of writing the one-line SQL fix.

During a production latency incident caused by a single bad deploy or missing database index, the agent may force a causal-loop/system map, speculate about retry storms or emergent behavior, and recommend architecture-level mitigations while delaying the obvious evidence-first actions: inspect traces, compare deploy timelines, rollback, or fix the query/index.

boundaries: When the problem has a known recent change (deploy, config, migration) — check the diff first, not the system map
When the issue is reproducible and localizable to a single component through standard instrumentation — debug the component, not the system
Under active incident time pressure — mitigate first, map feedback loops after the outage
When the system is simple enough (low interconnectivity, few feedback paths) that component-level root cause analysis suffices
When the agent lacks observability into the interconnections — you can't do systems analysis without data on the links

Organizational/process problems: the skill mentions these but provides only technical metaphors (circuit breakers, queues, caches). An agent could model a team conflict as 'resource contention' and recommend 'sharding' — a category error with real consequences
When the observed behavior IS explainable by a single component's known failure mode — emergence isn't the default explanation, it's the explanation of last resort
Fails to establish a complexity floor—agents might apply this to standard CRUD tasks just because they involve a client, API, and DB.
Does not tell the agent when to STOP mapping the system and start executing the fix.
Lacks distinction from 'thinking-feedback-loops' and 'eng-holistic-systems', likely causing redundant skill invocations.
Do not use for isolated deterministic bugs, syntax errors, local regressions, or single-component failures with clear evidence.
Do not use as a substitute for logs, metrics, traces, reproduction, or change-history analysis.
Do not force a feedback loop when the evidence only supports a one-way causal chain.
Do not recommend large paradigm or architecture changes before testing smaller reversible interventions.
Do not apply to organizational or team dynamics without evidence; it can encourage speculative psychoanalysis.
During urgent incidents, stabilize first with rollback, mitigation, or containment before broad systems analysis.

fixes.fix: Replace the over-broad decision flow with a diagnostic gate: 'Have you exhausted component-level debugging and the behavior still doesn't make sense? Does the behavior change when you change one part in ways that can't be explained by that part alone? If no to both, debug the component — you don't need systems thinking yet.' This prevents the skill from becoming a hammer that makes every multi-service problem look like a nail. Additionally, add Gall's Law as a counterweight: 'A complex system that works evolved from a simple system that worked' — reminding the agent that sometimes the right move is simplification, not richer analysis of complexity.

Remove the ASCII diagram templates entirely and replace them with a text-based, structured relationship mapping protocol. Add a strict boundary explicitly instructing the agent NOT to use this for deterministic, localized bugs.

Add an evidence-first boundary section: separate observed facts from hypotheses, rule out simpler local causes first, require measurements for each causal link, and treat feedback loops as falsifiable models rather than mandatory artifacts.
